title: 第 26 節
en: 2 J. Prinsep says : " The Roman denarius, from which Dinar was derived, was itself of silver, while the Persian Dirhem (a silver coin) represents the Drachma, or dram weight, of the Greeks. The weight allowed to the Dinar of 32 ratis, or 64 grains, agrees so closely with the Roman and Greek unit of 60 grains, that its identity cannot be doubted, especially when we have before us the actual gold coins of Chandragupta (?) (didrachmas), weighing from 120 to 130 grains, and indubitably copied from Greek originals, in device as well as weight."
r 3 246 SUTRA PERIOD.

narius is not of so late a date in India as is generallysupposed. Yet the earliest document where it occurs is the Sanchi inscription JSTo. I.1 Burnouf remarked that he never found the word dindra used in what he considered the ancient Buddhist Sutras. It occurs in the Avadana-sataka, and in the Divyavadana. It would seem to follow, therefore, either that the Unadi-sutras and Panini must be placed later than Chandragupta, or that the Sutra in which this word is explained is spurious. It would not be right to adopt the latter supposition without showing some cause for it. It is well known that in a literature which is chiefly preserved by oral tradition, corrections and additions are more easily admitted than in works existing in MS. The ancient literature of India was continually learnt by heart ; and even at the present day, when MSS. have become so common, some of its more sacred portions must still be acquired by the pupil from the mouth of a teacher, and not from MSS. If new words, therefore, had been added to the language of India after the first composition of the Unadisutras, there would be nothing surprising in a Sutra being added to explain such words. Happily, however, we are not left in this instance to mere hypothesis. Ujjvaladatta, the author of a commentary on the Unadi-sutras, forms a favourable exception to most Sanskrit commentators, in so far as he gives us in his Commentary some critical remarks on the readings of MSS. which he consulted. He states in his introduction that he had consulted old MSS. and commentaries, and he evidently feels conscious of the merit of his work, when i Journal A. S. B., vol. vi. p. 455. Notes on the facsimiles of the inscriptions from Sanchi near Bhilsa, by James Prinsep.

SUTRA PERIOD. 247 he says, " If anybody, after having studied this commentary of mine, suppresses my name in order to put forth his own power, his virtuous deeds will perish."1 Now in his remarks on our Sutra, Ujjvaladatta says, " Dinara means a gold ornament, but this Sutra is not to be found in the Sutivritti and DevaVl*itti3a If, therefore, the presence of this word in the Unadi-sutras would have overthrown our calculations as to the age of Panini and his predecessor who wrote the Sutras, the absence of it except in one Sutra, which is proved to be of later date, must serve to confirm our opinion. Cosmas Indicopleustes remarked that the Roman denarius was received all over the world ; and how the denarius came to mean in India a gold ornament we may learn from a passage in the " Life of Mahavira."3 There it is said that a lady had around her neck a string of grains and golden dinars, and Stevenson adds that the custom of stringing coins together, and adorning with them children especially, is still very common in India.

That Ujjvaladatta may be depended upon when he makes such statements with regard to MSS. or commentaries, collated by himself, can be proved by another instance. In the Unadi-Sutras IV. 184, we read: " kritrikripibhyah kitan." Out of the three words of which the etymology is given in this Sutra, Icripitam, water, and kiritam, a crest, are known as ancient words. The former occurs in the Gana 2 w^fot 3?fanft (*mterh ?)\^rr^r ^ *r t7^ ii 3 Kalpa-sutra, translated by Stevenson, p. 45.
k 4 248 SUTRA PERIOD.

Kripanadi (Pan. VIII. 2. 18. 1.); the other in the Gana arddharchadi. The third word, however, tirita, a tiara, has never been met with in works previous to Panini. Now, with regard to this word, Ujjvaladatta observes that it is left out in the Nyasa.1 The authority of this work, a commentary by Jinendra on the K&sikavritti, would, by itself, be hardly of sufficient weight ; but on referring to the MS. of Mahabhashya at the Bodleian Library, I find that there also the Sutra is quoted exactly as Ujjvaladatta said, l; e. without the root from which tirita is derived. Having thus found Ujjvaladatta trustworthy and accurate in his critical remarks, we feel inclined to accept his word, even where we cannot control him, or where the presence of certain words in the Sutras might be explained without having recourse to later interpolations. Thus stupah, which occurs III. 25, might be explained as simply meaning a heap of earth. Nay, it is a word which, in its more general sense, is found in the Veda. Yet the most common meaning of stupa is a Buddhist monument, and as we are told by Ujjvala, that this word does not occur in the Sativritti, and that in the Sarvasva it is derived in a different manner, we can have little doubt that it was not added till after the general Colebrooke, Miscellaneous Essays, ii. 40, mentions this work in his list of Sanskrit grammars : " Nyasa or Kasika vritti panjika by Jinendra : another exposition of the Kasika vritti, with explanatory notes by Rakshita." He adds, however, with his usual caution : " I state this with some distrust, not having yet seen the book. The Nyasa is universally cited ; and the Bodhinyasa is frequently so. Vopadeva's Kavyakamadhenu quotes the Nyasa of Jinendra and that of Jinendrabuddhi."

PARISISHTAS. 249 spreading of Buddhism and the erection of Topes in India ; a negative argument which gives additional strength to the supposition that the original Unadisutras were composed before that period.1 To add one more instance. In all the editions of the Unadi-sutras, Jina occurs as the name of the founder of a Bauddha sect. As many scholars have assigned to Jina and the Jains a very modern date, the presence of this name might seem to throw considerable doubt on the antiquity ascribed to the Unadi-sutras. In a passage of Say ana, however (Rv. i. 61. 4.), where he has occasion to quote the Sutra containing, among other words, the etymology of Jina, all the MSS. omit the root ji, from which Jina is said to be derived. It is equally omitted in Nrisinha's Svaramanjari.

The test which has thus been applied to our chronological arrangement of the Sutra literature in general, in the case of the Unadi-sutras, so far from invalidating, has rather strengthened our argument for placing the whole literature of the Sutras, at least of those which are connected with the Vedas, between the years 600 and 200 B.C.
Parisishtas.

There is one class of works which must be mentioned before we leave the Sutra period, the so-called Parisishtas. They are evidently later than the Sutras, and their very name, Paralipomena, marks their secondary importance. They have, however, a character of their own, and they represent a distinct period of Hindu literature, which, though it is of 1 The word stupa does not occur in Panini or the Ganapatha. Sayana to Rv. i. 24. 7. does not quote the Unadi-siitra, but derives stupa from a root styai, affix pa.

less interest to the student, and though it shows clear traces of intellectual and literary degeneracy, is not on that account to be overlooked by the historian. Some of the more substantial Parisishtas profess to be composed by authors whose names belong to the Sutra period. Thus iSaunaka is called the author of the Charanavyuha by the commentator of Paraskara's Grihya-sutras, Rama-krishna 1 (MS. E.T.H. 440. 577. 912.) ; a writer no doubt quite untrustworthy where he gives his own opinions, but yet of some importance where he quotes the opinions of others. Katyayana is quoted as the author of the Chhandogaparisishta.2 The same Kusika, who is known as the author of the Sutras for the Atharvana, is mentioned as the author of the Atharvana-parisishtas also. Other Parisishtas, though not ascribed to Katyayana, are said to be composed in accordance with his opinions.3 Again, while the Grihya-sutras of the Chhandogas are acknowledged as the work of Gobhila, a Parisishta on the same subject is ascribed to the son of Gobhila.4 The names of Saunaka and Katyayana are frequently invoked at the beginning or end of these works, and though some of them ap- 3 MS. Bodl. W. 510. ^"gT^TI TtftftlTf% Tf^V^t *Tq*f- ^r^^u^ll TARISISHTAS. 251 pear to us simply useless and insipid, it is not to be denied that others contain information which we should look for in vain in the Sutras. Their style is less concise than that of the Sutras. The simple Anushtubh Sloka preponderates, and the metre is more regular than that of the genuine Anushtubh compositions of Saunaka. Their style resembles that of the Barhaddaivata and Rig-vidh&na, works originally composed by Saunaka, but handed down to us, as it would seem, in a more modern form. But on the other side the Parisishtas have not yet fallen into that monotonous uniformity which we find in works like the Manava-dharma-s&stra, the Paddhatis, or the later Puranas ; and passages from them are literally quoted in the Pur&nas. The Parisishtas, therefore, may be considered the very last outskirts of Vedic literature, but they are Yedic in their character, and it would be difficult to account for their origin at any time except the expiring moments of the Yedic age.

The following argument may serve to confirm the favourable view which I take of some of the Parisishtas. Besides the MSS. of the Charanavyuha, there is a printed edition of it in Raja RMhakanta Deva's Sabdakalpadruma. This printed text is evidently taken from more modern MSS. It quotes seventeen instead of fifteen Sakh&s of the Yajasaneyins ; whereas the original number of fifteen is confirmed by our MSS. of the Charanavyuha, by the Pratijna-parisishta, and even by so late a work as theYishnu-purana (p. 281.). We may therefore suppose that at the time when the Parisishta, called the Charanavyuha, was originally composed, these two additional Sakhas did not yet exist. Now one of them is the Sakha of the Katyayaniyas, a Sakha, like many of those men- 252 PARISISHTAS.

tioned in the Puranas, founded on Sutras, not on Brahmanas. The fact, therefore, of this modern Sakha not being mentioned in the original Charanavyuha serves as an indication that at the time of the original composition of that Parisishta, sufficient time had not yet elapsed to give to Katyayana the celebrity of being the founder of a new Sakha.

On the other hand it should be stated that Panini does not seem to have known literary works called Parisishtas.1 The number of Parisishtas is frequently stated at eighteen. This may have been their number at some time, or for one particular Veda, but it is now considerably exceeded. The Charanavyuha, itself a Parisishta, gives the same number; but it seems to speak of the Parisishtas of the Yajur-veda only. There is a collection of Parisishtas for each Veda. Works, such as the Bahvricha-parisishta, Sankhayana-parisishta, Asvalayana-grihya-parisishta, must be ascribed to the Rig-veda. A MS, (Bodl. 466.) contains a collection of Parisishtas which belong to the Samaveda. At the end of the first treatise it is said : " iti Samaganam chhandah samaptam," " here end the metres of the Sama-singers." 2 Other treatises begin with the invocation, " Namah Samavedaya." The second is called Kratusangraha, on sacrifices; the third, Viniyoga-sangraha, on the employment of hymns ; the fourth, Somotpattih, on the origin of Soma. The fifth and sixth treatises contain the index to the Archika of the Sama-veda after the Naigeyasakha. As no pointed allusions to other Yedas occur 1 Parisishta occurs only as a pratyudaharana in Pan. iv. 1. 48, but it is used there as a feminine, and in quite a different sense.
2 It is also called chhandasain vichayah, and contains quotations from the Tandya-brahmana, Pingala,the Nidana, and Ukthasastra.

PARISISHTAS. 253 in these tracts, there can be little doubt that the whole collection of these Parisishtas may be classed as Sama-veda literature. The Chhandoga-parisishta, however, which is commonly ascribed to Katyayana, is not found in this MS. The Parisishtas of the Yajurveda are enumerated in the Charanavyuha, and will have to be examined presently. Those of the Atharvana are estimated by Professor Weber at seventyfour1, and are said to be written in the form of dialogues, in a style similar to that of the Puranas, and sometimes, we are told, agreeing literally with chapters of the astrological Sanhitas.
According to the Charanavyuha2 the following are the eighteen Parisishtas of the Yajur-veda:
1. The Yupalakshanam ; according to Yyasa's Charanavyuha, the Upajyotisham.
2. The Chhagalakshanam ; Mangalalakshanam, (Yyasa).
3. ThePratijna; Pratijnanuvakyam ? (Yyasa).
4. The Anuvakasankhya\ ; Parisankhya (Yyasa).
5. The Charanavyuhah ; Charanavyiihah (Yyasa).
6. The Sraddhakalpah ; Sraddhakalpah (7yasa).
7. The Sulvikani or SuMni.
8. The Parshadam.
1 According to a passage in the Charanavyuha, belonging to the Atharvana, the number of the Kausikoktani Parisishtani would amount to 70.

2 Besides the MS. of the E. I. H., and collations of some of the MSS. at Berlin, I have used the printed edition of the Charanavyuha in Radhakanta's Sanskrit Encyclopaedia. The MSS. differ so much that it would be hazardous to correct the one by the other. They probably represent different versions of the same text. The name of the author varies likewise. Sometimes he is called 6aunaka, sometimes Katyayana, and in Radhakanta's edition, Vyasa. The last is, perhaps, meant for the same whom we found mentioned before as the author of a Commentary on Patanj ali's Yoga. The text has since been published by Prof. Weber.
9. The Rigyajunshi.
10. The Ishtakapuranam.
zh: J. Prinsep 說：「羅馬德那里烏斯（denarius）是第納爾（Dinar）的來源，本身是白銀鑄幣，而波斯第爾罕（Dirhem，白銀鑄幣）則代表希臘人的德拉克馬（Drachma）或德拉姆衡量制。第納爾允許的重量為 32 拉提（ratis）或 64 格令（grains），與羅馬和希臘 60 格令的單位如此接近，其同一性毋庸置疑，尤其當我們擁有旃陀羅笈多（Chandragupta）(?)的實際金幣（二德拉克馬）在前，其重量在 120 至 130 格令之間，無可置疑地模倣自希臘原型，無論在圖案與重量方面皆然。」
r 3 246 蘇特拉時期。

那納里烏斯（narius）在印度的出現時間並非普遍認為的那麼晚近。然而，最早記載這個詞彙的文獻是桑奇銘文。¹布爾諾夫（Burnouf）指出，他從未在自己認為屬於古代佛教經典的文獻中發現「金德拉」（dindra）一詞的用法。該詞出現在《本生譚百則》（Avadana-sataka）和《法句譬喻經》（Divyavadana）中。由此似乎可以推出以下兩種可能性之一：要麼應該將《無迪蘇特拉》（Unadi-sutras）和帕尼尼（Panini）的著作置於旃陀笈多（Chandragupta）之後，要麼解釋這個詞彙的經文是虛假的。未能提出充分的理由便採納後者的假設是不恰當的。眾所周知，在主要由口頭傳統保存的文獻中，修正與增補較之於手稿本形式的著作更易被接受。印度古代文獻向來由人誦習；即使至今，當手稿本已變得如此普遍之時，其更為神聖的部分仍須由弟子直接聆聽教師的傳授，而非從手稿本中習得。因此，若在《無迪蘇特拉》初成之後有新詞彙被添加到印度語言中，則添加一條新的經文以解釋此等詞彙便沒有什麼令人驚訝的了。幸運的是，在此例中我們並非僅憑假設而論。《無迪蘇特拉》注疏作者烏日瓦剌達塔（Ujjvaladatta）在多數梵文注疏者中堪稱一個有利的例外，蓋其在其注疏中就所查閱手稿本的文本提供了某些批判性的評論。他在導言中聲稱曾查閱過古代手稿本與注疏，並且當他為自己的著作之價值感到自覺意識時， ¹ 《印度亞洲學會期刊》（Journal A. S. B.），第六卷，第 455 頁。詹姆斯‧普林塞普（James Prinsep）所著《比爾薩附近桑奇銘文臨摹本注疏》（Notes on the facsimiles of the inscriptions from Sanchi near Bhilsa）。

他說：「如果有人在研習了我的這部注疏後，隱沒我的名字以彰顯自己的力量，他的德行將消亡。」¹ 現在烏焦瓦拉達塔（Ujjvaladatta）在評註我們的蘇特拉時說：「迪那羅（Dinara）意為黃金飾品，但這個蘇特拉並未見於《蘇特拉吠利蒂》（Sutivritti）和《提婆吠利蒂》（DevaVitti）³。」因此，若這個詞語出現在《烏納迪蘇特拉》（Unadi-sutras）中本應推翻我們對於帕尼尼（Panini）及其撰寫蘇特拉的前任者年代的推算，那麼它除了在一部被證明為較晚成書的蘇特拉中出現外別無他處，這一事實必然會確認我們的看法。印度通科斯瑪斯（Cosmas Indicopleustes）曾註意到羅馬第納律斯幣（denarius）在整個世界流通；而迪那羅在印度如何變成了黃金飾品之義，我們可以從《大雄傳》（Life of Mahavira）³中的一段記載得到啟發。文中記載某位貴婦人頸項上掛著一串穀粒和黃金迪那羅幣，史蒂文森補充說，將硬幣串起來並用其裝飾兒童的習俗在印度至今仍極為普遍。

烏殊瓦拉達塔（Ujjvaladatta）在涉及他本人校勘過的手稿或注疏時所作的陳述是可以信賴的，這一點可以由另一個例子得到證明。在《尼地經》第四卷第184條中，我們讀到：「kritrikripibhyah kitan」。在這條經文中所給出詞源的三個詞彙之中，即 Icripitam（水）和 kiritam（冠冕）是已知的古代詞彙。前者出現在根據司蒂文森（Stevenson）翻譯的《劫波經》第45頁的《詞根表》第2節中。
k 4 248 經文時代。

克里帕納迪（Pan. VIII. 2. 18. 1.）；另一個在甘納阿爾德哈爾恰迪（Gana arddharchadi）中。然而第三個詞彙蒂里塔（tirita），意為冠冕，在帕尼尼之前的著作中從未出現過。關於這個詞彙，烏吉瓦拉達塔注意到它在《尼亞薩》（Nyasa）中被省略了。¹ 這部作品——即金德拉（Jinendra）對《喀什迦韻律》（Kāśikavṛtti）的評註——本身的權威性不足以令人信服；但當我參考博德萊安圖書館的《大評述》（Mahabhashya）手稿時，我發現那裡蘇特拉的引文也完全如烏吉瓦拉達塔所說的那樣，即不含衍生蒂里塔一詞的詞根。既然已經發現烏吉瓦拉達塔在其批判性評論中是可信且準確的，即使在無法驗證他的觀點之處，或在蘇特拉中某些詞彙的出現可以不必訴諸後代插補而得以解釋之處，我們也傾向於接納他的說法。是故斯圖帕（stupah），出現於第三卷第二十五條，可能僅被解釋為堆積的土堆。誠然，這是一個詞彙，在其更為廣泛的意義上存在於《吠陀》中。然而斯圖帕最常見的含義是佛教紀念塔，且據烏吉瓦拉所告，這個詞彙並未出現在《薩蒂韻律》（Sativritti）中，且在《薩瓦薩瓦》（Sarvasva）中它以不同的方式衍生，我們幾乎沒有理由懷疑它直到一般……之後才被加入。 柯爾布魯克《雜論集》第二卷第四十頁提及他的梵文語法著作清單中的這部作品：「《尼亞薩》或《喀什迦韻律評註》，金德拉著：《喀什迦韻律》的另一種闡釋，由拉克希塔作的說明註釋。」然而他附加說，帶著他一貫的謹慎態度：「我帶著某種懷疑陳述此事，尚未見過此書。《尼亞薩》被普遍引用；《波地尼亞薩》也經常被如此引用。沃帕德瓦的《詩藝蜜樹》（Kavyakamadhenu）引用了金德拉的《尼亞薩》和金德拉跋提的著作。」

帕里西什塔斯。249 佛教的傳播與印度塔廟的建立；這是一項消極的論證，進一步加強了原始烏那地經（Unadi-sutras）成書於此時期之前的推論。1 再舉一例。在烏那地經的所有版本中，「勝者」（Jina）作為某個佛教派系創始人的名號出現。許多學者將「勝者」（Jina）與耆那教信徒（Jains）定年為相當晚近的時期，這個名號的存在似乎會對烏那地經所謂的古老性投下相當大的疑影。然而，在薩耶那注疏中（梨俱吠陀 i. 61. 4.），他在引用包含「勝者」（Jina）詞源解釋在內的經文時，所有的手抄本都省略了據說是「勝者」所由來的字根 ji。在那羅辛哈的《聲韻珠寶》（Svaramanjari）中亦同樣省略此字根。
這項測試雖然應用在我們對蘇特拉（Sutra）文獻整體的年代安排上，在烏那地蘇特拉（Unadi-sutras）的例子中，非但沒有推翻我們的論證，反而進一步強化了我們的主張，即：將整個蘇特拉文獻，至少是那些與吠陀相關的蘇特拉，置於公元前六百年至二百年之間。
帕里西什塔斯。

在我們離開經文時期之前，必須提及一類著作，即所謂的《附篇》（Parisishtas）。它們顯然晚於《經文》，其名稱本身「補遺」（Paralipomena）就標誌著它們次等的重要性。然而，它們具有自己獨特的性質，代表印度文學的一個獨特時期，儘管它的……1 「塔」（stupa）一詞不見於帕尼尼或蓋那帕塔。娑耶那對《梨俱吠陀》i. 24. 7 的注釋未引用《烏那地經》（Unadi-siitra），而是將 stupa 衍生自根詞 styai 和詞尾 pa。

對學生來說興趣較少，雖然其中清楚展現出智識與文學的衰退跡象，但史學家仍不應因此而忽視之。一些較為重要的帕里希什陀經（Parisishta）聲稱由蘇特拉時期的作者撰述。例如，往世書注疏家羅摩奎師那在帕若斯卡拉的家庭儀軌經（Paraskara's Grihya-sutras）注疏中將紹那卡稱為《迴旋經》（Charanavyuha）的作者¹（MS. E.T.H. 440. 577. 912.）；此人在表述己見時固然難堪信任，但在引述他人意見之時，仍具有某種價值。迦坦耶那被推為《韻律帕里希什陀經》（Chhandogaparisishta）的作者。²同一位庫希卡（Kusika），作為阿闥婆吠陀經的蘇特拉作者而為人所知，也被提及為《阿闥婆帕里希什陀經》（Atharvana-parisishtas）的作者。其他帕里希什陀經雖未歸於迦坦耶那，卻據稱乃按其意見所撰述。³再者，雖然韻律派的家庭儀軌經（Grihya-sutras）被公認為歌毗羅的著作，但同一主題的帕里希什陀經卻歸於歌毗羅之子。⁴紹那卡與迦坦耶那的名字經常在這些著作的開篇或結尾被援引，雖然其中某些對我們而言只是無用而乏味，但不可否認其他一些包含了我們在蘇特拉中無法尋得的資訊。其文風不如蘇特拉那般簡潔。簡單的阿紐什圖普頌體（Anushtubh Sloka）佔主導地位，其格律比紹那卡的真正阿紐什圖普作品更為規律。其文風類似於《廣大天神讚》（Barhaddaivata）與《梨俱吠陀經儀軌》（Rig-vidh&na）——這些著作原為紹那卡所撰，但據推似乎已以更為現代的形式流傳至我們。但另一方面，帕里希什陀經尚未陷入我們在《摩奴法論》（Manava-dharma-s&stra）、《指南集》（Paddhatis）或後世往世書（Puranas）等著作中所見的單調一致性；其中的段落在往世書中被逐字引述。因此，帕里希什陀經可視為吠陀文獻的最後邊界，但其性質仍屬吠陀，其起源除卻吠陀時代的最後時刻外，實難以其他時間來說明。

以下論證可用來確認我對某些《帕里西塔》（Parisishtas）所持的有利看法。除了《查拉那毘由訶》（Charanavyuha）的手抄本外，在羅闍·摩訶甘塔·德瓦（Raja Mhakanta Deva）的《詞根寶庫》（Sabdakalpadruma）中還有一部印刷版本。這部印刷本顯然取自較為現代的手抄本。它引述的是雅舍內耶派（Yajasaneyins）的十七個（而非十五個）《沙卡》（Sakh&s）；而十五個的原始數目則由我們的《查拉那毘由訶》手抄本、《普提吉那帕里西塔》（Pratijna-parisishta），甚至《毘濕奴往世書》（Yishnu-purana，第281頁）這樣一部晚期著作所確認。因此我們可以推測，在原先撰寫名為《查拉那毘由訶》的《帕里西塔》的時代，這兩個額外的《沙卡》尚未存在。其中之一是迦帝亞耶尼亞派（Katyayaniyas）的《沙卡》，這是一個《沙卡》，正如許多這類人所──252 帕里西塔。
在《往世書》（Puranas）中被提及，是以《經文》（Sutras）為基礎而建立，而非以《梵書》（Brahmanas）為基礎。因此，這個現代《分支》（Sakha）未在原始的《頌法匯編》（Charanavyuha）中被提及這一事實，足以表明在該《附則》（Parisishta）原始成書的時代，尚未經歷足夠的時間跨度，使迦帝亞那（Katyayana）獲得新《分支》創立者的聲譽。

另一方面，應該指出的是，帕尼尼（Panini）似乎並未知悉名為《帕里希什塔》（Parisishtas）的文獻著作。¹《帕里希什塔》的數量經常被說為十八部。這可能曾是它們在某個時期的數量，或就某一部吠陀而言的數量，但現在已大大超過。《車隆那韋由哈》（Charanavyuha）本身是一部《帕里希什塔》，給出了相同的數字；但它似乎僅言及耶柱吠陀（Yajur-veda）的《帕里希什塔》。每一部吠陀都有一套《帕里希什塔》的彙編。諸如《百頌帕里希什塔》（Bahvricha-parisishta）、《三迦耶那帕里希什塔》（Sankhayana-parisishta）、《阿什瓦拉耶那家庭禮儀帕里希什塔》（Asvalayana-grihya-parisishta）等著作，應歸屬於梨俱吠陀。一份手稿（Bodl. 466.）包含了屬於娑摩吠陀的《帕里希什塔》彙編。在第一部論著的末尾記載：「iti Samaganam chhandah samaptam」，意為「此為娑摩歌者的韻律之終」。² 其他論著以「Namah Samavedaya」的祈禱文開始。第二部被稱為《祭禮摘要》（Kratusangraha），論述祭祀；第三部《讚頌應用摘要》（Viniyoga-sangraha），論述讚美詩的應用；第四部《蘇摩起源》（Somotpattih），論述蘇摩的起源。第五和第六部論著包含了按耐格耶沙卡分派的娑摩吠陀《梨俱集》（Archika）的索引。由於沒有出現對其他吠陀的明確指涉¹《帕里希什塔》（Parisishta）僅在《帕尼尼經》（Pan.）四卷一章四十八節作為對應物（pratyudaharana）出現，但在那裡被用作陰性詞，且含義完全不同。
它也被稱為 *chhandasain vichayah*（韻律的探究），並包含了來自《讚陀耶梵書》(*Tandya-brahmana*)、皮毘迦羅(*Pingala*)、《尼達那》(*Nidana*) 及《頌經論》(*Ukthasastra*) 的引文。

帕里希什塔經。253 在這些文獻中，幾乎毫無疑問，整個帕里希什塔經（Parisishta）的收藏可歸類為娑摩吠陀（Sama-veda）文獻。然而，通常歸因於卡提亞耶那（Katyayana）的《讚歌帕里希什塔》（Chhandoga-parisishta）在此手稿中未曾發現。夜柔吠陀（Yajurveda）的帕里希什塔經在《儀式綱領》（Charanavyuha）中被列舉，將在稍後進行檢視。阿闥婆吠陀（Atharvana）的帕里希什塔經據韋伯教授估計有七十四部¹，據說是以對話形式寫成，其風格類似於往世書（Purana），有時我們得知，其措辭會與占星學《本集》（Sanhita）的篇章逐字相符。
根據《聲聞儀軌》（Charanavyuha），以下為夜柔吠陀（Yajur-veda）的十八部《附則》（Parisishtas）：
《尤帕羅剎那摩》（Yupalakshanam）；根據毘耶娑（Vyasa）的《遮羅尼毘耶烏訶》（Charanavyuha），為《烏帕焦帝沙姆》（Upajyotisham）。
2. 《七行文》(Chhagalakshanam)；《吉祥文》(Mangalalakshanam)，(耶亞薩)。
3. 普拉蒂吉那（Pratijna）；普拉蒂吉那努瓦基亞（Pratijnanuvakyam）？（耶亞薩〔Yyasa〕）。
4. 《經文數目經》(Anuvakasankhya)；《節數經》(Parisankhya，毘耶娑著)。
5. 《住處布列經》(Charanavyuhah；Charanavyūhah)（毗耶娑著）
6. 《信心儀軌》；《信心儀軌》（7yasa）。
7. 《蘇爾維迦尼》或《蘇姆尼》（Sulvikani or SuMni）。

The Parshadam (Sanskrit पर्षदम्, *parṣadam*) literally means 'gift,' and is frequently used in the Vedic literature to denote those offerings which are made by men to the gods, and which, if accepted by the gods, are supposed to produce a reciprocal gift in return. This system of reciprocal gifts between gods and men is the very foundation of the Vedic religion, and has given rise to that peculiar form of theology which I have ventured to call **kathenotheism** (轮換主神教). The principle is simple. Whenever the Vedic poets approached a god with a prayer and a gift, they addressed him as if he were the supreme and only god. The god, if he accepted the offering, was supposed to bestow his favours in return. This is the reason why the same god is sometimes invoked with the highest titles of supremacy and godhead in one hymn, and in another hymn is invoked in much more humble terms. --- 帕沙達姆（梵文 पर्षदम्，*parṣadam*）字面上的意思是「禮物」，在吠陀文獻中頻繁出現，用以指代人類獻祭給諸神的祭品，如果諸神接受了這些祭品，就被認為會產生相應的回禮。這一神人之間的互惠禮物制度構成了吠陀宗教的基礎，並衍生出我大膽稱之為**輪換主神教**（kathenotheism）的獨特神學形式。 其原則甚為簡單。每當吠陀詩人帶著祈禱和祭品來朝拜某位神祇時，他們都會將其視為至高且唯一的神。若該神接受了祭品，就被認為會予以回報和恩澤。這正是為何同一位神在某一首讚美詩中被以最至高的主權和神聖的稱號來祈願，而在另一首讚美詩中卻以更為謙卑的措辭來祈願的原因所在。
根據屬於《阿闥婆吠陀》（Atharvana）的《儀軌經》（Charanavyuha）中的一段文字，《考西卡頌讚補遺》（Kausikoktani Parisishtani）的數量應達七十部。

2 除了《東方學報》(E. I. H.)的手稿以及柏林若干手稿的對校本外，我還使用了拉達坎塔《梵文百科全書》中印行的《護火儀軌》(Charanavyuha)版本。這些手稿差異甚大，以其中一部來校正另一部將是莽撞之舉。它們可能代表同一文本的不同版本。作者名字亦有差異。有時稱為紹納卡(Saunaka)，有時稱為卡提亞亞納(Katyayana)，在拉達坎塔的版本中則為毗耶娑(Vyasa)。最後這個名字，或許指的是我們前面提及過的帕坦伽利(Patañjali)《瑜伽經》註疏作者。該文本其後已由韋伯(Prof. Weber)教授出版。
9. 梨俱夜柔奴濕
10. 《伊什塔卡普拉南》
